questions.question: RAG addresses which LLM limitation primarily?
options: Knowledge cutoff and hallucination for domain-specific Q&A
questions.explanation: RAG retrieves current, relevant documents at query time -- grounding the LLM in real information rather than training-time knowledge.

questions.question: Chunking in RAG refers to:
options: Splitting documents into smaller pieces for indexing and retrieval
questions.explanation: Full documents are too long to embed meaningfully. Chunking (100-500 tokens, ~20% overlap) creates retrievable units.

questions.question: Chunk overlap in text splitting helps because:
options: It preserves context at chunk boundaries -- sentences are not cut mid-thought
questions.explanation: Without overlap, a sentence split across chunk boundary might be retrieved incomplete. Overlap ensures boundary sentences appear in full in at least one chunk.

questions.question: In RAG, the retrieval step uses:
options: Vector similarity search between query embedding and document embeddings
questions.explanation: Both dense (vector) and sparse (BM25) retrieval are common. Hybrid = dense + sparse. The query is embedded and compared to all chunk embeddings.

questions.question: Context precision measures:
options: What fraction of retrieved chunks are actually relevant to the query
questions.explanation: High precision = retrieved chunks are relevant. Low precision = noisy context that confuses the LLM.

questions.question: The main difference between RAG and fine-tuning is:
options: RAG changes model weights, fine-tuning doesn't
RAG retrieves knowledge at inference time, fine-tuning bakes it into model weights
Fine-tuning works for all knowledge types
questions.explanation: RAG = dynamic, updateable, traceable. Fine-tuning = static knowledge in weights, can't be updated without retraining.

questions.question: Reranking in advanced RAG pipelines:
options: Applies a cross-encoder to re-score top-k retrieved chunks for relevance
questions.explanation: Bi-encoder (dense retrieval) is fast but approximate. Cross-encoder reranker scores query-doc pairs jointly -- slower but much more accurate.

questions.question: Sentence-transformers produce:
options: Sentence/paragraph level embeddings optimised for semantic similarity
questions.explanation: Sentence-transformers fine-tune transformers with contrastive objectives to produce embeddings where similar sentences are close in vector space.

questions.question: Retrieval augmentation reduces hallucination because:
options: The LLM is instructed to answer only from provided context -- factual claims are grounded
questions.explanation: 'Answer using only the provided context. If not there, say I don't know.' -- this instruction forces grounded responses.

questions.question: RAGAS evaluates RAG systems on which dimensions?
options: Context Precision, Context Recall, Answer Faithfulness, Answer Relevancy
questions.explanation: RAGAS provides automated LLM-as-judge metrics for all four dimensions -- enabling dataset-level RAG quality measurement without human annotation.
